serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chye sim soup with minced pork is approximately 400 g, typically served as a light accompaniment to rice in a Vietnamese or Singaporean home-style meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Mustard greens, cooked, boiled (ID 11271) and Pork, ground, cooked (ID 10219)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chye sim soup with minced pork found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked mustard greens (USDA FDC ID 11271) and cooked ground pork (USDA FDC ID 10219) in approximate proportions consistent with a light Vietnamese-style soup (roughly 60% broth/water, 25% chye sim, 15% minced pork by weight), cross-referenced against the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input values were retained where consistent with component-level estimates. Sodium reflects fish sauce addition. Values are estimates; a laboratory analysis or HPB-verified entry would improve confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — vegetable soup analogous reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chye sim soup with minced pork. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on analogous vegetable-dominant broth soups in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., vegetable soup GI ~30–40, low-carbohydrate broth soups). The dish contains only 2.25 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from chye sim; the protein and fibre content further blunts glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (32 × 9.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.9, rounded to 1.6 per 100 g equivalent for template field (GL per serving = 32 × (2.25 × 4 / 100) = 32 × 0.09 = 2.9 per 400 g bowl). GL per serving reported as 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 237.5 mg per 100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing ~41% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and salt during preparation, opt for homemade low-sodium broth, and limit portion to half a bowl to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.25 g/100 g) and low sugar (0.5 g/100 g) with moderate protein (4.5 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (0.75 g/100 g) produce minimal glycaemic impact; estimated GI ~32 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes as a low-GI, low-carbohydrate dish; pair with a small portion of brown rice rather than white rice to maintain overall meal glycaemic control, and monitor sodium from fish sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.75 g/100 g and the dish is boiled (no added frying oils), but sodium at 237.5 mg/100 g is a concern for cardiovascular risk when consumed in full-bowl portions.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and boiled preparation make this dish heart-friendly in terms of fat profile; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium preparation and limit portion size to manage cumulative daily sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chye sim provides vitamin C (6.25 mg/100 g) and antioxidants; minced pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which may amplify inflammatory pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Keep the pork portion modest relative to the vegetable content, request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and minimise cooking time to preserve chye sim's anti-inflammatory sulforaphane content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chye Sim Soup with Minced Pork (Vietnamese: Canh Cải Thịt Băm) is a light, clear broth-based soup popular in Vietnamese home cooking and found across Vietnamese-Singaporean households and food stalls. The dish features chye sim (菜心, Chinese mustard greens, Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis) — a leafy green with a mildly bitter, slightly sweet flavour — simmered in water or pork broth with small amounts of seasoned minced pork, fish sauce, garlic, and salt. The preparation method is simple boiling or simmering, which preserves the bright green colour and tender-crisp texture of the chye sim while allowing the pork to release umami into the broth. The resulting soup is savoury, lightly flavoured, and clean-tasting, typically served alongside steamed rice as part of a multi-dish Vietnamese or Singaporean family meal. Nutritionally, the soup is low in calories (~45 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (2.25 g/100 g), and provides moderate protein (4.5 g/100 g) primarily from the minced pork. Saturated fat is low (0.75 g/100 g) and the dish contains no trans fat. Sodium is moderate at 237.5 mg/100 g, largely attributable to fish sauce; a full 400 g bowl delivers approximately 950 mg sodium. Chye sim contributes vitamin C (6.25 mg/100 g), calcium (20 mg/100 g), iron (0.38 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (0.75 g/100 g). The estimated glycemic index is low (~32), making this soup appropriate for glycaemic management. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content, but sodium should be monitored for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. It is dairy-free and gluten-free (when fish sauce is gluten-free), making it accessible to multiple dietary needs.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this dish was found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked mustard greens and cooked ground pork in proportions consistent with a light Vietnamese-style soup, cross-referenced against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). The GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup references. Confidence is medium because the component-level estimation is methodologically sound but has not been verified by laboratory analysis or a direct approved-source entry for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (cooked mustard greens FDC ID 11271; cooked ground pork FDC ID 10219) in proportions consistent with a light Vietnamese-style soup, cross-referenced against the input ai_generated values. A direct HPB Foodo or laboratory-verified entry would improve confidence to high. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources for this estimated dish): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and moisture values are not reliably derivable from the available approved sources for this specific composite dish preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.8) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for cooked ground pork and cooked mustard greens; these are approximate. 4. potassium_mg (200.0) is estimated from chye sim and pork component data; not directly measured. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for boiled vegetable and lean pork preparation with no hydrogenated fats. 6. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup references; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = approximately 2.9 g. 7. The dish is not certified halal due to pork content. 8. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting only 'menu_listing' and has been replaced with approved primary sources for nutrient data.
